---
# ============================================================
#  MODULE 2 LECTURE SLIDES — SOURCE FILE
#  PAF 516 | Community Analytics | Arizona State University
#  Render to PDF: quarto render Module2_Lecture_Slides.qmd
# ============================================================
title: "Cartographic Design"
subtitle: "Module 2 | PAF 516 — Community Analytics"
author: "Arizona State University"

format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: beaver
    fonttheme: professionalfonts
    slide-level: 2
    aspectratio: 169
    toc: false
    section-titles: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \definecolor{asuMaroon}{HTML}{8C1D40}
        \definecolor{asuGold}{HTML}{FFC627}
        \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{title}{fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{alerted text}{fg=asuMaroon}
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
        \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{size=\large,series=\bfseries}

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Part 1: Classification & Design

## The Core Question

> "Does the story your map tells change depending on how you classify the data?"

**Answer: Yes — and the differences can be dramatic.**

In Module 1 you built the index and produced a basic choropleth. This module asks harder questions:

- Which **classification method** best represents your data?
- Can one map display **two variables simultaneously**?
- Do your conclusions survive a **change of geographic unit**? *(MAUP)*
- What separates a **professional map** from default output?

**We move from making maps → designing visual arguments.**

## Module Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

1. Apply and compare **four classification methods**: quantile, Jenks, equal interval, standard deviation
2. Explain how classification affects **map interpretation and policy conclusions**
3. Create a **bivariate choropleth** using the `biscale` package
4. Build an **interactive mapgl map** with tooltips and hover effects
5. Apply cartographic design principles: **visual hierarchy**, figure-ground, color ramps
6. Recognize and account for the **Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP)**

## Classification Methods

Every choropleth requires a decision: how to divide continuous data into discrete color bins?

::: {.callout-important}
This is **not a neutral technical choice** — it is an **editorial decision**.
:::

| Method | How It Works | Best For | Risk |
|--------|-------------|----------|------|
| **Equal Interval** | Equal-width bins | Uniform distributions | Skewed data → one bin dominates |
| **Quantile** | Equal observations per bin | Visual variation | Groups very different values |
| **Jenks Natural Breaks** | Minimize within-class variance | Natural clusters | Not comparable across datasets |
| **Std. Deviation** | Bins centered on mean (±1SD) | Deviation from average | Assumes near-normal distribution |

## Brewer & Pickle (2002)

> *Brewer, C. A., & Pickle, L. (2002). Evaluation of methods for classifying epidemiological data on choropleth maps in series. Annals of the AAG, 92(4), 662–681.*

The **definitive empirical study** of classification method performance (~1,700 citations):

- **Quantile and natural breaks outperform equal interval** for map-reading accuracy
- Equal interval performed worst when data distributions are skewed — common in census data
- Classification method affects **policy interpretation**: same data, different conclusions
- No single method is universally best

::: {.callout-warning}
**"I used the default" is not a justification.** You must be able to defend your classification choice.
:::

## Choosing a Classification Method

| Your Goal | Best Method | Why |
|-----------|-------------|-----|
| Show relative position | Quantile | Equal visual weight per class |
| Reveal natural clusters | Jenks Natural Breaks | Minimizes within-class variance |
| Compare to a benchmark | Standard Deviation | Deviation from mean is the story |
| Communicate absolute thresholds | Equal Interval | Bins have intuitive meaning |
| Compare across multiple maps | Equal Interval or SD | Consistent bins enable fair comparison |

**Tip:** Examine your histogram before choosing. Run all 4 methods and compare — then justify.

## Color Theory for Maps

**Sequential** (light → dark)
: For ordinal/continuous data (poverty rate, economic hardship index). Example: viridis, ColorBrewer Blues.

**Diverging** (two hues from neutral midpoint)
: For data with a meaningful center (change scores, deviation from mean). Example: blue–white–red.

**Qualitative** (distinct hues, equal visual weight)
: For categorical data with no implied order. Example: county type, administrative region.

::: {.callout-tip}
**ColorBrewer 2.0** (colorbrewer2.org) — all palettes tested for colorblind safety, print friendliness, and screen legibility.
:::

## Bivariate Choropleth Maps

A bivariate choropleth maps **two variables simultaneously** using a 3×3 color grid:

- `bi_class()` — classifies two variables into a combined column (e.g., "1-1", "2-3")
- `bi_scale_fill()` — applies the 2D color palette to ggplot
- `bi_legend()` — generates the 3×3 legend (compose with map using patchwork)

::: {.callout-note}
Stevens (2015): "Keep it 3×3. Color selection and legend design are the two most critical decisions in bivariate mapping."
:::

## Cartographic Design Principles

**Visual Hierarchy** — Most important data = most visually prominent. Basemap labels should recede.

**Figure-Ground** — Study area stands out from surrounding context.

**Color Choice** — Sequential → ordinal. Diverging → centered. Qualitative → categorical. Always colorblind-safe.

**Required Map Elements** — Every thematic map needs: title, legend, scale bar, north arrow, data source citation.

> Before opening R, answer these 5 questions *(Axis Maps Cartography Guide)*:
> 1. What is the message?  2. Who is the audience?  3. What data type?  4. What is the distribution?  5. Where will it be shown?

## Small Multiples

Small multiples place the same map design side-by-side with one element varied. The eye does the comparison.

**When to use:**

- **Classification comparison** — map same data with 4 methods
- **Temporal comparison** — map 2015 and 2023 side-by-side
- **Variable comparison** — map all 5 index components
- **Scale comparison** — county → tract → block group *(illustrates MAUP)*

In R: `patchwork` makes small multiples trivial:
```r
p1 + p2 + p3 + plot_annotation(title = "Classification Comparison")
```
*(Walker, 2023, Chapter 6)*

---

# Part 2: The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem

## What Is MAUP?

> *Openshaw, S. & Taylor, P.J. (1979). A million or so correlation coefficients: three experiments on the modifiable areal unit problem. Statistical Applications in the Spatial Sciences, pp. 127–144.* (~1,700 citations — coined the term)

**The foundational finding:** Using Iowa county data, aggregating 99 counties into 6-zone configurations produced correlations ranging from **−0.97 to +0.99**.

**Two components:**

- **Scale Effect** — Results change as data are aggregated into progressively larger units
- **Zonation Effect** — Results change with alternative boundary configurations at the SAME scale

::: {.callout-important}
MAUP means there is **no single "correct" set of areal units**. Every boundary system is arbitrary and modifiable.
:::

## Openshaw (1984) — The Full Framework

> *Openshaw, S. (1984). The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem. CATMOG No. 38. Geo Books.* (~5,200 citations — most-cited MAUP reference)

Key contributions:

- Traced MAUP back to Gehlke & Biehl (1934) — the problem predates GIS by decades
- Connected MAUP to the **ecological fallacy**: area-level correlations ≠ individual-level relationships
- Showed correlations can be "manufactured" across the full range from −1 to +1
- Proposed the **Automatic Zoning Procedure (AZP)** as a computational partial solution

> **Openshaw's warning:** *"Areal units are arbitrary, modifiable, and subject to the whims and fancies of whoever did the aggregating."*

## MAUP in Multivariate Analysis

> *Fotheringham, A.S. & Wong, D.W.S. (1991). The modifiable areal unit problem in multivariate statistical analysis. Environment and Planning A, 23(7), 1025–1044.* DOI: 10.1068/a231025

Fotheringham and Wong extended MAUP from bivariate correlations into **multiple regression and logit models**:

- Parameter estimates varied by a factor of up to **nine** across geographic units
- MAUP effects in multivariate contexts are **"essentially unpredictable in their intensity and effects"**
- Coefficient signs can **reverse** depending on the aggregation unit chosen
- The regressions you run on census tracts give different results at county or block group level

::: {.callout-warning}
No mathematical correction fully resolves MAUP in multivariate analysis. **Awareness and sensitivity checks are the primary defenses.**
:::

## MAUP in Choropleth Mapping

> *Nelson, J.K. & Brewer, C.A. (2017). Evaluating data stability in aggregation structures across spatial scales. Cartography and Geographic Information Science, 44(1), 35–50.* DOI: 10.1080/15230406.2015.1093431

The modern cartographic treatment of MAUP:

- Used LISA statistics to measure how spatial patterns change across Census aggregation levels
- Tested median income (PA) and cancer rates (NY) at block group, tract, and county
- **Census-tract level data preserves statistical relationships far better than county-level data**
- Aggregating to county suppresses meaningful neighborhood variation
- Directly bridges MAUP theory and choropleth map design practice

**For PAF 516:** This is why Lab 1 maps hardship at county, tract, and block group. The patterns — and the policy conclusions — depend on which scale you choose.

## Census Geographies and MAUP

| Geography | ~N in AZ | Reveals | Limitation |
|-----------|----------|---------|------------|
| **US County** | ~3,100 nationally | Broad regional patterns; tribal areas, urban cores | Suppresses within-county variation |
| **Census Tract** | ~1,700 in AZ | Neighborhood-level variation; urban/suburban gradient | Some MOE at small population tracts |
| **Block Group** | ~900 in Maricopa | Finest ACS geography; block-by-block variation | Highest margins of error |

**Recommended for most analyses:** Census tract.

**Used for LISA hot spots:** Block group — denser spatial units improve clustering detection.

## Classification + Scale Together

Two compounding sources of map uncertainty:

| Combination | What You See |
|------------|-------------|
| Equal Interval + County | Broad national patterns; within-county variation invisible |
| Jenks + Block Group | Neighborhood clusters revealed; local inequality visible |
| Quantile + Census Tract | Balanced visual variation; good for most purposes |
| Std. Deviation + County | Only extreme outliers stand out; middle class flattened |

> **A single index. Four combinations. Four different policy narratives.**
> Choose deliberately and report transparently.

## Why MAUP Matters for Policy

Real-world consequences of ignoring MAUP:

- **Redistricting & gerrymandering** — Legislative boundaries are constructed to produce specific outcomes
- **Enterprise zone targeting** — Which neighborhoods qualify depends on the geographic unit and classification method chosen
- **Health disparity research** — Fotheringham & Wong showed income-health regression coefficients can reverse sign across geographic units
- **School district analysis** — District-level poverty rates mask extreme within-district variation

::: {.callout-tip}
**Practical guidance:** Always report which geographic unit you used and justify the choice. Run sensitivity checks at multiple scales when possible.
:::

## Module 2 Summary

**Key takeaways:**

- **Classification is editorial.** Brewer & Pickle (2002): quantile and Jenks outperform equal interval for most census data.
- **Color carries meaning.** Use ColorBrewer 2.0 for tested, accessible palettes.
- **Two variables, one map.** Bivariate choropleths reveal relationships single-variable maps miss. Keep it 3×3.
- **MAUP: scale is not neutral.** Openshaw & Taylor (1979); Openshaw (1984): the same data aggregated differently produces different results.
- **MAUP is unpredictable.** Fotheringham & Wong (1991): parameter estimates can vary 9×. Awareness is the defense.
- **Census geography matters.** Nelson & Brewer (2017): tract-level data preserves relationships that county-level analysis destroys.

**Coming up in Module 3:** Spatial Data Integration — spatial joins, buffer analysis, and layering external datasets onto the economic hardship index.
